questions.question: At equilibrium, the reaction $\text{N}_2(g) + 3\text{H}_2(g) \rightleftharpoons 2\text{NH}_3(g)$ has $K_c = 6.0 \times 10^{-2}$. Which of the following correctly interprets this $K_c$ value?

choices.text: Products are strongly favored at equilibrium because $K_c > 0$
choices.explanation: All K values are positive; K > 0 alone does not indicate strong product favorability. K_c = 0.06 < 1, indicating reactants are favored at equilibrium.

choices.text: Reactants are favored at equilibrium because $K_c < 1$
choices.explanation: When K < 1, the equilibrium position lies toward the reactants; at equilibrium, the concentration of reactants is greater than products.

choices.text: The reaction is complete (goes to completion) because $K_c$ is defined
choices.explanation: A defined K does not mean the reaction goes to completion; K_c = 0.06 means significant reactant concentrations remain at equilibrium.

choices.text: The reaction rate is slow because $K_c$ is small
choices.explanation: K describes the equilibrium position, not the reaction rate; kinetics and thermodynamics (equilibrium) are independent concepts.

questions.subject: ap-chemistry

questions.question: For the reaction $\text{CO}(g) + 3\text{H}_2(g) \rightleftharpoons \text{CH}_4(g) + \text{H}_2\text{O}(g)$, what is the expression for $K_c$?

choices.text: $K_c = \dfrac{[\text{CO}][\text{H}_2]^3}{[\text{CH}_4][\text{H}_2\text{O}]}$
choices.explanation: This expression has reactants over products; the correct equilibrium expression has products over reactants.

choices.text: $K_c = \dfrac{[\text{CH}_4][\text{H}_2\text{O}]}{[\text{CO}][\text{H}_2]^3}$
choices.explanation: For the equilibrium expression, products appear in the numerator raised to their stoichiometric powers, and reactants in the denominator: K_c = [CH₄][H₂O] / ([CO][H₂]³).

choices.text: $K_c = \dfrac{[\text{CH}_4][\text{H}_2\text{O}]}{[\text{CO}][\text{H}_2]}$
choices.explanation: H₂ has a stoichiometric coefficient of 3 in the balanced equation; its concentration must appear cubed in the equilibrium expression.

choices.text: $K_c = [\text{CH}_4][\text{H}_2\text{O}] - [\text{CO}][\text{H}_2]^3$
choices.explanation: The equilibrium constant is a ratio (products/reactants), not a difference; the correct form is a fraction.

questions.subject: ap-chemistry

stimulus.content: A student prepares a reaction mixture for $\text{N}_2\text{O}_4(g) \rightleftharpoons 2\text{NO}_2(g)$ with initial concentrations $[\text{N}_2\text{O}_4] = 0.50$ mol/L and $[\text{NO}_2] = 0$ mol/L. After reaching equilibrium, $[\text{N}_2\text{O}_4] = 0.40$ mol/L.

questions.question: What is the equilibrium concentration of $\text{NO}_2$?

choices.text: 0.10 mol/L
choices.explanation: 0.10 mol/L is the change in [N₂O₄]; since the stoichiometric ratio of N₂O₄:NO₂ is 1:2, [NO₂] = 2 × 0.10 = 0.20 mol/L.

choices.text: 0.20 mol/L
choices.explanation: ICE table: N₂O₄ decreased by 0.50 − 0.40 = 0.10 mol/L. By stoichiometry, 2 mol NO₂ forms per mol N₂O₄ consumed: [NO₂] = 2(0.10) = 0.20 mol/L.

choices.text: 0.40 mol/L
choices.explanation: 0.40 mol/L is the equilibrium concentration of N₂O₄, not NO₂; NO₂ is the product formed from N₂O₄ dissociation.

choices.text: 0.50 mol/L
choices.explanation: 0.50 mol/L was the initial concentration of N₂O₄, not the equilibrium concentration of NO₂; the amount of NO₂ formed is twice the decrease in N₂O₄ concentration.

questions.subject: ap-chemistry

questions.question: At 500°C, $K_c = 0.040$ for the reaction $\text{H}_2(g) + \text{I}_2(g) \rightleftharpoons 2\text{HI}(g)$. If the initial concentrations are $[\text{H}_2] = [\text{I}_2] = 0.10$ mol/L and $[\text{HI}] = 0$ mol/L, what is the reaction quotient Q, and in which direction will the reaction proceed?

choices.text: $Q = 0$; the reaction proceeds forward (toward products)
choices.explanation: Q = [HI]²/([H₂][I₂]) = 0²/(0.10)(0.10) = 0/0.01 = 0. Since Q < K_c, the reaction proceeds forward to form more HI until Q = K_c.

choices.text: $Q = 0.040$; the reaction is already at equilibrium
choices.explanation: Q = 0 because [HI] = 0 initially; Q = 0 ≠ K_c = 0.040, so the system is not at equilibrium.

choices.text: $Q = \infty$; the reaction proceeds in reverse (toward reactants)
choices.explanation: Q would be infinite only if [H₂] or [I₂] were zero while [HI] was non-zero; with [HI] = 0 initially, Q = 0.

choices.text: $Q = 0.010$; the reaction proceeds in reverse
choices.explanation: Q = 0.010 would require [HI]² = 0.010 × 0.01 = 1.0 × 10⁻⁴, meaning [HI] = 0.01 mol/L; initially [HI] = 0, so Q = 0.

questions.subject: ap-chemistry

questions.question: Which of the following changes will shift the equilibrium to the right (toward more $\text{NH}_3$)?

choices.text: Increasing temperature
choices.explanation: The forward reaction is exothermic; increasing temperature shifts equilibrium toward the endothermic (reverse) direction, producing less NH₃.

choices.text: Decreasing pressure by increasing volume
choices.explanation: The forward reaction decreases moles of gas (4 → 2); decreasing pressure (increasing volume) shifts equilibrium toward more moles of gas (reverse direction, producing N₂ and H₂).

choices.text: Increasing the concentration of $\text{N}_2$
choices.explanation: Adding more reactant (N₂) increases Q relative to K; to re-establish equilibrium, the forward reaction is favored, producing more NH₃.

choices.text: Adding an inert gas at constant volume
choices.explanation: Adding an inert gas at constant volume does not change the partial pressures or concentrations of reactants or products; equilibrium is not affected.

questions.subject: ap-chemistry

questions.question: For the dissolution equilibrium $\text{AgCl}(s) \rightleftharpoons \text{Ag}^+(aq) + \text{Cl}^-(aq)$, the $K_{sp} = 1.8 \times 10^{-10}$. What is the molar solubility of $\text{AgCl}$ in pure water?

choices.explanation: Let s = molar solubility. [Ag⁺] = [Cl⁻] = s. K_sp = s² = 1.8 × 10⁻¹⁰; s = √(1.8 × 10⁻¹⁰) = 1.34 × 10⁻⁵ mol/L.
K_sp = s² for a 1:1 salt; taking the square root of K_sp gives s = √(1.8 × 10⁻¹⁰) = 1.34 × 10⁻⁵ mol/L, not K_sp itself.
9.0 × 10⁻¹¹ = K_sp/2; dividing K_sp by 2 is not the correct method. Molar solubility is obtained by taking the square root of K_sp for a 1:1 salt.
This value (2 × K_sp) does not arise from any correct calculation of molar solubility; s = √K_sp = 1.34 × 10⁻⁵ mol/L.

questions.subject: ap-chemistry

questions.question: Based on the table, which of the following statements about equilibrium is best supported?

choices.text: Changing temperature is unique because it is the only perturbation that changes the value of K
choices.explanation: K is a constant that depends only on temperature; concentration changes, pressure changes, and catalysts shift the equilibrium position but do not change K. Only temperature changes alter K.

choices.text: A catalyst shifts equilibrium to the right because it speeds up the forward reaction more than the reverse
choices.explanation: A catalyst speeds up both forward and reverse reactions equally, lowering the activation energy of both; it does not shift the equilibrium position.

choices.text: Decreasing product concentration always increases K because products appear in the denominator of K
choices.explanation: Products appear in the numerator of K; moreover, K changes only with temperature, not with concentration changes.

choices.text: Increasing reactant concentration permanently increases K because Q > K
choices.explanation: Increasing reactant concentration temporarily makes Q < K, shifting equilibrium forward, but K itself remains unchanged at constant temperature.

questions.subject: ap-chemistry

questions.question: For the reaction $\text{PCl}_5(g) \rightleftharpoons \text{PCl}_3(g) + \text{Cl}_2(g)$, what happens to the equilibrium position when the total pressure is increased by decreasing the volume at constant temperature?

choices.text: Equilibrium shifts to the right because the pressure increase favors more moles of gas
choices.explanation: The Le Châtelier response to increased pressure is to shift toward the side with fewer moles of gas; the right side has 2 moles vs. 1 mole on the left, so equilibrium shifts left.

choices.text: Equilibrium shifts to the left because there are fewer moles of gas on the left side
choices.explanation: Increased pressure favors the side with fewer moles of gas. Left side: 1 mol PCl₅; right side: 1 mol PCl₃ + 1 mol Cl₂ = 2 mol. Equilibrium shifts left.

choices.text: Equilibrium is unaffected because pressure changes do not affect equilibrium
choices.explanation: Pressure changes do affect equilibrium for gas-phase reactions with different numbers of moles on each side; pressure has no effect only when Δn_gas = 0.

choices.text: Equilibrium shifts to the right because PCl₅ is more dense and settles under pressure
choices.explanation: Density and settling do not determine equilibrium shifts; Le Châtelier's principle based on moles of gas is the correct framework.

questions.subject: ap-chemistry

stimulus.content: The reaction $2\text{SO}_2(g) + \text{O}_2(g) \rightleftharpoons 2\text{SO}_3(g)$ has $K_c = 280$ at 1000 K. A reaction mixture has $[\text{SO}_2] = 0.40$ mol/L, $[\text{O}_2] = 0.30$ mol/L, and $[\text{SO}_3] = 3.0$ mol/L.

questions.question: Which of the following correctly determines the direction the reaction will proceed to reach equilibrium?

choices.text: Q > K_c, so the reaction proceeds in reverse (toward reactants)
choices.explanation: Q = (3.0)^2/((0.40)^2(0.30)) = 9.0/0.048 = 187.5. Since Q = 187.5 < K_c = 280, the reaction quotient is less than the equilibrium constant, so the reaction proceeds forward, not in reverse.

choices.text: Q < K_c, so the reaction proceeds forward (toward products)
choices.explanation: Q = (3.0)^2/((0.40)^2(0.30)) = 187.5. Since Q (187.5) < K_c (280), the system has not yet reached equilibrium and must proceed in the forward direction to increase Q toward K_c.

choices.text: $Q = K_c$, so the system is already at equilibrium
choices.explanation: Q = 188 ≠ 280 = K_c; the system is not at equilibrium.

choices.text: Q < K_c, so the reaction proceeds in reverse (toward reactants)
choices.explanation: When Q < K_c, the reaction proceeds forward (more products form); when Q > K_c, the reaction proceeds in reverse.

questions.subject: ap-chemistry

questions.question: The common-ion effect predicts that adding $\text{NaCl}$ to a saturated solution of $\text{AgCl}$ will:

choices.text: Increase the molar solubility of AgCl by providing a common ion that increases ionic strength
choices.explanation: Adding a common ion (Cl⁻) shifts the dissolution equilibrium to the left, decreasing the solubility of AgCl.

choices.text: Decrease the molar solubility of AgCl by providing $\text{Cl}^-$ ions that shift the dissolution equilibrium to the left
choices.explanation: NaCl provides additional Cl⁻ ions; Q becomes > K_sp, so AgCl precipitates until a new, lower equilibrium solubility is established.

choices.text: Have no effect because NaCl and AgCl are both ionic compounds
choices.explanation: The chemical nature of NaCl vs. AgCl is not the determining factor; the common Cl⁻ ion directly participates in the AgCl equilibrium.

choices.text: Convert AgCl to NaCl by exchanging ions, increasing the total dissolved ion concentration
choices.explanation: No such ion exchange occurs between these salts in solution; the equilibrium shift is due to the common-ion effect, not an exchange reaction.

questions.subject: ap-chemistry

questions.question: Based on the data in the table for the reaction $\text{A}(g) + \text{B}(g) \rightleftharpoons \text{C}(g) + \text{C}(g)$, what is the value of $K_c$ at equilibrium?

choices.explanation: 0.50 is the equilibrium concentration of each species, not K_c; K_c = [C]² / ([A][B]) = (0.50)² / (0.50)(0.50) = 0.25/0.25 = 1.0.
K_c = 2.0 would require [C]²/([A][B]) = 2.0; with equal concentrations of 0.50 mol/L, K_c = (0.50)²/(0.50)² = 1.0.
0.25 = (0.50)² but this ignores the denominator; K_c = [C]²/([A][B]) = 0.25/0.25 = 1.0, not 0.25.

questions.subject: ap-chemistry

questions.question: Which of the following correctly relates $K_c$ and $K_p$ for the reaction $\text{CaCO}_3(s) \rightleftharpoons \text{CaO}(s) + \text{CO}_2(g)$?

choices.text: $K_p = K_c(RT)^{-1}$ because $\Delta n_{gas} = -1$
choices.explanation: CO₂ is the only gas produced (1 mol gas in products, 0 in reactants); Δn_gas = +1, so K_p = K_c(RT)^{+1} = K_c · RT.

choices.text: $K_p = K_c \cdot RT$ because $\Delta n_{gas} = 1$
choices.explanation: Solids are excluded from K; K_c = [CO₂] and K_p = P_CO₂. Since there is 1 mole of gas produced and 0 consumed, Δn_gas = 1, and K_p = K_c(RT)^1 = K_c · RT.

choices.text: $K_p = K_c$ because the solids cancel out of the equilibrium expression
choices.explanation: While solids are excluded from K expressions, K_p and K_c are still related by (RT)^Δn; with Δn_gas = 1, they are not equal unless RT = 1.

choices.text: $K_p = K_c$ because there is only one gaseous species
choices.explanation: The relationship K_p = K_c(RT)^Δn_gas depends on Δn_gas, not the number of gaseous species; with Δn_gas = 1, K_p ≠ K_c.

questions.subject: ap-chemistry

stimulus.content: A slightly soluble salt $\text{Mg}(\text{OH})_2$ has $K_{sp} = 5.6 \times 10^{-12}$. A solution of $\text{MgCl}_2$ is prepared with $[\text{Mg}^{2+}] = 0.10$ mol/L.

questions.question: What is the maximum concentration of $\text{OH}^-$ that can be present before $\text{Mg}(\text{OH})_2$ begins to precipitate?

choices.explanation: K_sp = [Mg²⁺][OH⁻]² = (0.10)(x)² = 5.6 × 10⁻¹²; x² = 5.6 × 10⁻¹¹; x = 7.5 × 10⁻⁶ mol/L, not K_sp itself.
K_sp = [Mg²⁺][OH⁻]². At the precipitation threshold: (0.10)[OH⁻]² = 5.6 × 10⁻¹²; [OH⁻]² = 5.6 × 10⁻¹¹; [OH⁻] = 7.5 × 10⁻⁶ mol/L.
5.6 × 10⁻¹¹ = K_sp/[Mg²⁺] = [OH⁻]²; taking the square root: [OH⁻] = 7.5 × 10⁻⁶ mol/L.
2.4 × 10⁻⁶ does not result from the correct K_sp calculation; the correct answer requires taking the square root of K_sp/[Mg²⁺].

questions.subject: ap-chemistry

questions.question: Le Châtelier's principle predicts that for an endothermic reaction, increasing temperature will:

choices.text: Shift equilibrium to the left and decrease K
choices.explanation: For an endothermic reaction, heat is a reactant; adding heat shifts equilibrium to the right (toward products), increasing K.

choices.text: Shift equilibrium to the right and increase K
choices.explanation: In an endothermic reaction, heat is on the reactant side; increasing temperature 'adds reactant,' shifting equilibrium right. A higher T increases K for endothermic reactions.

choices.text: Not affect equilibrium because temperature does not change concentrations
choices.explanation: Temperature changes do affect equilibrium; unlike concentration or pressure changes, temperature changes alter K itself.

choices.text: Shift equilibrium to the right but decrease K
choices.explanation: For an endothermic reaction, increasing temperature both shifts equilibrium right AND increases K; these two outcomes are consistent, not contradictory.

questions.subject: ap-chemistry

stimulus.content: For the equilibrium $\text{Fe}^{3+}(aq) + \text{SCN}^-(aq) \rightleftharpoons \text{Fe(SCN)}^{2+}(aq)$, the product $\text{Fe(SCN)}^{2+}$ is deep red. A student observes a deep red solution at equilibrium, then adds $\text{KSCN}$ solid.

questions.question: Which of the following correctly predicts and explains the observation after adding KSCN?

choices.text: The solution becomes lighter (less red) because the equilibrium shifts right, consuming SCN⁻
choices.explanation: If equilibrium shifts right, more FeSCN²⁺ (red) forms, making the solution darker, not lighter.

choices.text: The solution becomes darker (more red) because adding SCN⁻ increases Q relative to K, shifting the equilibrium right to form more Fe(SCN)²⁺
choices.explanation: Adding SCN⁻ increases [SCN⁻]; Q = [FeSCN²⁺]/([Fe³⁺][SCN⁻]) temporarily changes; to restore K, more FeSCN²⁺ forms (equilibrium shifts right), deepening the red color.

choices.text: No color change occurs because KSCN is a solid and cannot affect equilibrium
choices.explanation: When KSCN dissolves, it releases SCN⁻(aq), which is a direct participant in the equilibrium; it definitely affects the equilibrium position.

choices.text: The solution becomes colorless because excess SCN⁻ decomposes the FeSCN²⁺ complex
choices.explanation: Excess SCN⁻ promotes forward reaction (forms more FeSCN²⁺), not decomposition; the color intensifies, not disappears.

questions.subject: ap-chemistry

questions.question: For the reaction $2\text{A}(g) \rightleftharpoons \text{B}(g) + \text{C}(g)$, $K_c = 4.0$. If [A] = 1.0 mol/L, [B] = 2.0 mol/L, and [C] = 2.0 mol/L, the reaction quotient Q is:

choices.text: Q = 1.0; reaction proceeds forward
choices.explanation: Q = [B][C]/[A]² = (2.0)(2.0)/(1.0)² = 4.0/1.0 = 4.0 = K_c; the system is at equilibrium, not driving forward.

choices.text: Q = 4.0; the system is at equilibrium
choices.explanation: Q = [B][C]/[A]² = (2.0)(2.0)/(1.0)² = 4.0 = K_c; Q = K_c means the system is at equilibrium.

choices.text: Q = 8.0; reaction proceeds in reverse
choices.explanation: Q = (2.0)(2.0)/(1.0)² = 4.0, not 8.0; the system is exactly at equilibrium.

choices.text: Q = 0.25; reaction proceeds forward

questions.subject: ap-chemistry

questions.question: When a solid is added to a saturated solution of a slightly soluble salt, the value of $K_{sp}$:

choices.text: Increases because more solid increases the concentration of ions
choices.explanation: Adding more solid to a saturated solution does not change the ion concentrations (equilibrium is already saturated); K_sp is a constant at fixed temperature.

choices.text: Decreases because the additional solid shifts the equilibrium to the left
choices.explanation: K_sp depends only on temperature, not on the amount of solid present; adding solid does not change K_sp.

choices.text: Remains constant because $K_{sp}$ depends only on temperature
choices.explanation: K_sp (like all equilibrium constants) is temperature-dependent only; adding more solid has no effect on K_sp, though it does not dissolve beyond the solubility limit.

choices.text: Changes depending on how much solid is added relative to the solution volume
choices.explanation: K_sp is independent of the amount of solid or solution volume; it is a thermodynamic constant at a given temperature.

questions.subject: ap-chemistry

stimulus.content: An industrial process uses the reversible reaction: $2\text{SO}_2(g) + \text{O}_2(g) \rightleftharpoons 2\text{SO}_3(g)$; $\Delta H = -198$ kJ

questions.question: A chemical engineer wants to maximize the yield of $\text{SO}_3$. Which combination of conditions is most favorable for high yield?

choices.text: High temperature and low pressure
choices.explanation: High temperature shifts exothermic equilibrium left (less SO₃); low pressure also shifts equilibrium left (products side has fewer moles: 2 SO₃ vs. 3 reactants).

choices.text: Low temperature and high pressure
choices.explanation: Low temperature favors the exothermic forward reaction (more SO₃). High pressure favors fewer moles of gas (2 SO₃ vs. 3 on reactant side). Both conditions maximize SO₃ yield thermodynamically.

choices.text: High temperature and high pressure
choices.explanation: High pressure is favorable, but high temperature shifts the exothermic equilibrium left; the net yield of SO₃ would be lower than at low temperature.

choices.text: Low temperature and low pressure
choices.explanation: Low temperature is favorable for yield, but low pressure shifts equilibrium toward the side with more moles of gas (reactants), reducing SO₃ yield.

questions.subject: ap-chemistry

questions.question: For the equilibrium $\text{H}_2(g) + \text{I}_2(g) \rightleftharpoons 2\text{HI}(g)$, $K_c = 50$ at 448°C. If the reaction is written in reverse as $2\text{HI}(g) \rightleftharpoons \text{H}_2(g) + \text{I}_2(g)$, what is $K_c$ for the reverse reaction?

choices.explanation: When a reaction is reversed, K_c becomes 1/K_c; the value for the reverse reaction is 1/50 = 0.020, not 50.
Reversing a reaction takes the reciprocal of K_c; K_c(reverse) = 1/K_c(forward) = 1/50 = 0.020.
K_c is always positive; a negative value has no physical meaning for an equilibrium constant.
Doubling the stoichiometric coefficients squares K_c (giving 2500); reversing the reaction takes the reciprocal (giving 0.020). 100 does not result from any correct operation here.

questions.subject: ap-chemistry

title.text: Concentration vs. Time for A ⇌ B

questions.question: Based on the concentration-time graph, which of the following correctly identifies when the system reaches equilibrium and what $K_c$ equals?

choices.text: Equilibrium is reached at approximately $t = 8$ min; $K_c = [\text{B}]/[\text{A}] = 0.60/0.40 = 1.5$
choices.explanation: Concentrations stop changing at t ≈ 8–10 min, indicating equilibrium. K_c = [B]/[A] = 0.60/0.40 = 1.5 (assuming the reaction A ⇌ B is first order in both directions).

choices.text: Equilibrium is reached at $t = 4$ min; $K_c = 0.55/0.45 = 1.2$
choices.explanation: At t = 4 min, concentrations are still changing (they continue to shift until t ≈ 8 min); equilibrium occurs when concentrations level off.

choices.text: Equilibrium is never reached because [A] and [B] have different values
choices.explanation: Equilibrium does not require equal concentrations; it requires constant (unchanging) concentrations over time, which occurs here at t ≈ 8 min.

choices.text: Equilibrium is reached at $t = 0$; $K_c = 0/1.0 = 0$
choices.explanation: At t = 0, only reactant A is present ([B] = 0); the system is far from equilibrium (Q = 0 < K_c). Equilibrium is reached later when concentrations level off.

questions.subject: ap-chemistry

questions.question: If $K_c$ for a reaction is $1.0 \times 10^{15}$, which of the following best describes the equilibrium position?

choices.text: Reactants strongly favored; very little product present at equilibrium
choices.explanation: K >> 1 means the numerator (products) greatly exceeds the denominator (reactants); products are strongly favored, not reactants.

choices.text: Products strongly favored; the reaction essentially goes to completion
choices.explanation: K_c = 10¹⁵ is extremely large; the equilibrium position lies so far to the right that the reaction effectively goes to completion, with negligible reactant remaining.

choices.text: Equal amounts of reactants and products at equilibrium
choices.explanation: Equal concentrations would give K ≈ 1; K = 10¹⁵ indicates overwhelmingly more product than reactant at equilibrium.

choices.text: The reaction is impossible because K_c cannot be that large
choices.explanation: K_c values span many orders of magnitude; 10¹⁵ is a large but chemically real value seen in highly thermodynamically favorable reactions.

questions.subject: ap-chemistry

stimulus.content: A student adds excess $\text{Ba}^{2+}$ to a solution containing $\text{SO}_4^{2-}$ and $\text{CrO}_4^{2-}$ ions. $K_{sp}(\text{BaSO}_4) = 1.1 \times 10^{-10}$; $K_{sp}(\text{BaCrO}_4) = 1.2 \times 10^{-10}$.

questions.question: Which of the following best predicts which compound precipitates first and why?

choices.text: $\text{BaSO}_4$ precipitates first because it has a smaller $K_{sp}$, meaning it is less soluble
choices.explanation: A smaller K_sp indicates lower solubility. BaSO₄ (K_sp = 1.1 × 10⁻¹⁰) is less soluble than BaCrO₄ (1.2 × 10⁻¹⁰); it precipitates at a lower [Ba²⁺] concentration.

choices.text: $\text{BaCrO}_4$ precipitates first because chromate has a higher charge density
choices.explanation: Charge density alone does not determine precipitation order; K_sp values determine which compound precipitates first when adding a common cation.

choices.text: Both precipitate simultaneously because $K_{sp}$ values are nearly equal
choices.explanation: While the K_sp values are close, they are not equal; BaSO₄ has a slightly smaller K_sp and begins to precipitate at a slightly lower Ba²⁺ concentration.

choices.text: Neither precipitates because the K_sp values are too small to exceed
choices.explanation: Small K_sp means low solubility and easy precipitation; adding Ba²⁺ will eventually cause Q > K_sp for both, leading to precipitation.

questions.subject: ap-chemistry

questions.question: Which of the following correctly explains the difference between the equilibrium constant $K$ and the reaction quotient $Q$?

choices.text: K applies only to gaseous reactions, while Q applies to all reactions
choices.explanation: Both K and Q can be written for any reaction (gaseous, aqueous, heterogeneous); the distinction is not about the phase of reactants.

choices.text: K is the value of the concentration/pressure ratio at equilibrium; Q is the same ratio calculated from any set of concentrations, not necessarily at equilibrium
choices.explanation: K is a constant (at fixed T) that equals the concentration ratio only at equilibrium. Q uses the same expression but can be calculated at any time; comparing Q to K predicts the direction of reaction.

choices.text: Q is always greater than K before equilibrium is reached
choices.explanation: Q can be either greater or less than K depending on initial conditions; Q < K means the reaction proceeds forward, Q > K means it proceeds in reverse.

choices.text: K depends on concentrations; Q depends on pressures for gas-phase reactions
choices.explanation: Both K_c and Q_c use concentrations; both K_p and Q_p use pressures; the K/Q distinction is not about concentrations vs. pressures.

questions.subject: ap-chemistry

questions.question: A reaction vessel contains $\text{H}_2(g)$, $\text{I}_2(g)$, and $\text{HI}(g)$ at equilibrium at 500°C. The volume of the vessel is suddenly doubled at constant temperature. Which of the following best predicts the new equilibrium position?

choices.text: Equilibrium shifts to the right because the pressure decrease favors more moles of gas
choices.explanation: H₂ + I₂ ⇌ 2HI has equal moles of gas on both sides (1+1=2 on left, 2 on right); Δn_gas = 0. Pressure changes have no effect on this equilibrium.

choices.text: Equilibrium shifts to the left because the volume increase dilutes the products more than the reactants
choices.explanation: With Δn_gas = 0, dilution affects all species equally; there is no net shift in equilibrium position when volume is changed for a reaction with equal gas moles on both sides.

choices.text: The equilibrium position does not change because the number of moles of gas is the same on both sides
choices.explanation: For H₂ + I₂ ⇌ 2HI, Δn_gas = 2 − (1+1) = 0. When Δn_gas = 0, pressure/volume changes do not shift the equilibrium; K_p = K_c for this reaction.

choices.text: The equilibrium position shifts to re-establish the same total pressure by forming more moles of gas
choices.explanation: Equilibrium shifts to restore the same K value, not the same pressure; with Δn_gas = 0, no shift occurs.

questions.subject: ap-chemistry

questions.question: The molar solubility of $\text{PbF}_2$ in pure water is $s = 2.1 \times 10^{-3}$ mol/L. What is $K_{sp}$ for $\text{PbF}_2$?

choices.explanation: 4.4 × 10⁻⁶ = (2.1 × 10⁻³)² is K for a 1:1 salt; PbF₂ gives 1 Pb²⁺ and 2 F⁻ per formula unit, so K_sp = s × (2s)² = 4s³.
9.3 × 10⁻⁹ is s³ without the stoichiometric factor; K_sp = [Pb²⁺][F⁻]² = s(2s)² = 4s³ = 4 × 9.26 × 10⁻⁹ = 3.7 × 10⁻⁸.
8.8 × 10⁻⁶ = 2(2.1 × 10⁻³)² = 2s²; this would apply to a 1:2 salt formula if K_sp = 2s², which is not the correct expression for PbF₂ (K_sp = 4s³).

questions.subject: ap-chemistry

questions.question: Based on the data in the table, which of the following best describes the meaning of a large equilibrium constant ($K \gg 1$)?

choices.text: The reaction proceeds quickly to products because the rate constant is large
choices.explanation: K (equilibrium constant) describes the position of equilibrium, not the speed of the reaction; rate constant (k) describes reaction speed. A large K means products are favored at equilibrium but says nothing about how quickly equilibrium is reached.

choices.text: At equilibrium, the concentration of products is much greater than the concentration of reactants
choices.explanation: K = [products]/[reactants] (for a simple reaction); when K ≫ 1, the numerator (products) dominates the denominator (reactants), meaning equilibrium lies far to the right with predominantly product concentrations.

choices.text: The forward reaction is exothermic and releases more energy than the reverse reaction
choices.explanation: A large K indicates the equilibrium position, not the energetics; an exothermic reaction can have a large or small K depending on the magnitude of ΔH° and the temperature.

choices.text: The reaction goes to completion and cannot reach equilibrium
choices.explanation: Even with a very large K, a reversible reaction still reaches equilibrium (just with very high product concentrations). Only irreversible reactions go to completion.

questions.subject: ap-chemistry

stimulus.content: For the reaction $\text{N}_2(g) + 3\text{H}_2(g) \rightleftharpoons 2\text{NH}_3(g)$, $K_c = 0.500$ at a certain temperature. A reaction mixture contains $[\text{N}_2] = 0.200$ M, $[\text{H}_2] = 0.300$ M, and $[\text{NH}_3] = 0.100$ M.

questions.question: Which of the following correctly calculates the reaction quotient $Q$ and predicts the direction the reaction will proceed to reach equilibrium?

choices.text: $Q = 0.185$; reaction proceeds forward (toward products) to increase $[\text{NH}_3]$
choices.explanation: The calculated Q is 1.85, not 0.185 — this is an arithmetic error (likely a misplaced decimal). $Q_c = [\text{NH}_3]^2 / ([\text{N}_2][\text{H}_2]^3) = (0.100)^2 / [(0.200)(0.300)^3] = 0.0100 / 0.00540 = 1.85$. Since Q = 1.85 > K_c = 0.500, the reaction proceeds in reverse, not forward.

choices.text: $Q = 1.85$; since $Q > K_c$, the reaction proceeds in reverse (toward reactants) to decrease $[\text{NH}_3]$
choices.explanation: $Q_c = [\text{NH}_3]^2 / ([\text{N}_2][\text{H}_2]^3) = (0.100)^2 / [(0.200)(0.300)^3] = 0.0100 / 0.00540 = 1.85$. Since $Q = 1.85 > K_c = 0.500$, there is too much product; the reaction shifts in reverse to reach equilibrium.

choices.text: $Q = 0.500$; the system is already at equilibrium and no shift occurs
choices.explanation: Q = 1.85 ≠ K = 0.500; the system is not at equilibrium. Q must be calculated from the given concentrations and compared to K.

choices.text: $Q = 1.85$; since $Q > K_c$, the reaction proceeds forward to increase $[\text{NH}_3]$
choices.explanation: When Q > K, the reaction has too much product relative to equilibrium; it proceeds in reverse (toward reactants) to reduce product concentrations and increase reactant concentrations until Q = K.

questions.subject: ap-chemistry

questions.question: Using the Le Chatelier's principle table, which of the following correctly predicts the effect on $K$ when the temperature is increased for an endothermic reaction?

choices.text: K decreases because increasing temperature for any reaction always decreases K
choices.explanation: The table shows K decreases only for exothermic reactions when temperature increases; for endothermic reactions, increasing temperature shifts equilibrium toward products, increasing K.

choices.text: K increases because increasing temperature shifts an endothermic equilibrium toward products
choices.explanation: For endothermic reactions, heat is a reactant; increasing temperature is equivalent to adding a reactant, shifting equilibrium toward products. This increases the ratio [products]/[reactants] at equilibrium, increasing K.

choices.text: K is unchanged because the table shows catalysts are the only factor that doesn't change K, and temperature change acts like a catalyst
choices.explanation: Temperature changes alter K because they change the thermodynamic equilibrium constant (ΔG° = −RT ln K); only temperature changes the value of K, not concentration or pressure changes.

choices.text: K increases only if the pressure is also increased simultaneously
choices.explanation: Pressure changes affect equilibrium position but not K; temperature is the only variable that changes K. The change in K for an endothermic reaction with increasing temperature is independent of pressure.

questions.subject: ap-chemistry

questions.question: For the equilibrium $2\text{SO}_2(g) + \text{O}_2(g) \rightleftharpoons 2\text{SO}_3(g)$, $K_c = 2.8 \times 10^2$ at 1000 K. Which of the following correctly writes the equilibrium expression $K_c$?

choices.explanation: The denominator in K_c uses the product of reactant concentrations (each raised to its stoichiometric coefficient), not their sum.

choices.text: $K_c = \frac{[\text{SO}_3]^2}{[\text{SO}_2]^2[\text{O}_2]}$
choices.explanation: K_c = [products raised to their coefficients] / [reactants raised to their coefficients]. For $2\text{SO}_2 + \text{O}_2 \rightleftharpoons 2\text{SO}_3$: $K_c = \frac{[\text{SO}_3]^2}{[\text{SO}_2]^2[\text{O}_2]}$.

choices.text: $K_c = \frac{[\text{SO}_2]^2[\text{O}_2]}{[\text{SO}_3]^2}$
choices.explanation: This is the expression for the reverse reaction; K_c for the forward reaction has products in the numerator and reactants in the denominator.

choices.text: $K_c = \frac{2[\text{SO}_3]}{2[\text{SO}_2] + [\text{O}_2]}$
choices.explanation: Stoichiometric coefficients become exponents in the equilibrium expression, not multipliers; K_c uses concentrations raised to their coefficients.

questions.subject: ap-chemistry

stimulus.content: For the reaction $\text{CO}(g) + 3\text{H}_2(g) \rightleftharpoons \text{CH}_4(g) + \text{H}_2\text{O}(g)$, a chemist places 0.100 mol CO and 0.300 mol H₂ in a 1.00 L flask at a given temperature. At equilibrium, $[\text{CH}_4] = 0.090$ M.

questions.question: Using an ICE table, which of the following correctly identifies the equilibrium concentrations?

choices.explanation: If CH₄ = 0.090 M was produced (x = 0.090), then H₂ decreased by 3x = 0.270 (not 0.030). [H₂] = 0.300 − 0.270 = 0.030 M is correct, but [CO] = 0.100 − 0.090 = 0.010 M.
H₂ must decrease by 3x = 3(0.090) = 0.270 (stoichiometric ratio H₂:CH₄ = 3:1); [H₂] = 0.300 − 0.270 = 0.030 M, not 0.210 M.
If CH₄ = 0.090 M was produced, CO and H₂ must have decreased by the stoichiometric amounts; the initial concentrations would only remain if no reaction occurred.

questions.subject: ap-chemistry

questions.question: The reaction $\text{PCl}_5(g) \rightleftharpoons \text{PCl}_3(g) + \text{Cl}_2(g)$ is exothermic. According to Le Chatelier's principle, which of the following changes would decrease the equilibrium concentration of $\text{PCl}_5$?

choices.text: Increasing the temperature
choices.explanation: The forward reaction is exothermic, so heat can be treated as a product: PCl₅ → PCl₃ + Cl₂ + heat. Increasing temperature adds heat, which shifts the equilibrium to the left (toward reactants), increasing [PCl₅] rather than decreasing it.

choices.text: Increasing the pressure by compressing the system
choices.explanation: The forward reaction produces more moles of gas (1 mol PCl₅ → 2 mol gas); increasing pressure shifts equilibrium left toward fewer gas moles, increasing [PCl₅].

choices.text: Adding an inert gas at constant volume
choices.explanation: Adding an inert gas at constant volume does not change the partial pressures of the reacting gases; equilibrium is unaffected and [PCl₅] does not change.

choices.text: Removing $\text{Cl}_2$ from the equilibrium mixture
choices.explanation: Removing a product (Cl₂) shifts the equilibrium to the right (toward products) to partially replace the removed species; this consumes PCl₅, decreasing [PCl₅].

questions.subject: ap-chemistry

questions.question: What is the molar solubility of AgCl in pure water at 25°C?

choices.explanation: 1.8 × 10⁻¹⁰ is K_sp itself; molar solubility s requires taking the square root: s = √K_sp = √(1.8 × 10⁻¹⁰) = 1.34 × 10⁻⁵ mol/L.
3.2 × 10⁻²⁰ = (1.8 × 10⁻¹⁰)² = K_sp²; molar solubility requires taking the square root of K_sp, not squaring it.
9.0 × 10⁻¹¹ = K_sp/2; this incorrectly divides K_sp by 2. For a 1:1 electrolyte like AgCl, s = √K_sp.

questions.subject: ap-chemistry

questions.question: Based on the graph, which of the following best describes the system at t = 15 s?

choices.text: The reaction has stopped because no more product is being formed after t = 15 s
choices.explanation: The reaction has not stopped; it has reached dynamic equilibrium. At equilibrium, forward and reverse rates are equal, resulting in constant concentrations, but both reactions continue to occur.

choices.text: The system has reached equilibrium; the forward and reverse rates are equal and concentrations remain constant
choices.explanation: The graph shows constant concentrations ([reactant] = 0.50 M, [product] = 0.25 M) from t = 15 s onward, which is the hallmark of dynamic equilibrium. Net concentration change = 0 because forward rate = reverse rate.

choices.text: The reactant is fully consumed; all material has converted to product
choices.explanation: The graph shows [reactant] = 0.50 M (not zero) at equilibrium; reactant is not fully consumed in a reversible reaction that reaches equilibrium.

choices.text: The reaction is second order because the concentrations stabilize after 15 s
choices.explanation: The stabilization of concentrations reflects equilibrium, not reaction order; reaction order describes the rate law and would need to be determined from initial rate data, not from when equilibrium is reached.

questions.subject: ap-chemistry

questions.question: For the reaction $\text{H}_2(g) + \text{I}_2(g) \rightleftharpoons 2\text{HI}(g)$, $K_c = 50$ at 448°C. If $[\text{H}_2] = [\text{I}_2] = 0.01$ M at equilibrium, what is the equilibrium concentration of HI?

choices.explanation: 0.50 would result from K_c × [H₂][I₂] = 50 × 0.01 = 0.50 without taking the square root; since [HI] is squared in K_c, the square root is required.
0.707 would result from using [H₂] = [I₂] = 0.1 M (not 0.01 M); with [H₂] = [I₂] = 0.01 M, the calculation gives √(50 × 0.0001) = √0.005 = 0.071 M.
0.005 = 50 × (0.01)² = 50 × 10⁻⁴ = [HI]², which is [HI] squared, not [HI] itself; taking the square root gives [HI] = 0.071 M.

questions.subject: ap-chemistry

stimulus.content: A student tests the common ion effect by measuring the solubility of $\text{AgCl}$ ($K_{sp} = 1.8 \times 10^{-10}$) in two solutions: pure water and 0.10 M NaCl.

questions.question: Which of the following correctly predicts the molar solubility of AgCl in 0.10 M NaCl and explains why it differs from the solubility in pure water?

choices.text: Solubility in 0.10 M NaCl ≈ $1.8 \times 10^{-9}$ mol/L; the common Cl⁻ ion shifts the equilibrium left, drastically reducing AgCl solubility
choices.explanation: With 0.10 M Cl⁻ already present: K_sp = [Ag⁺][Cl⁻] = s × (0.10 + s) ≈ s × 0.10 = 1.8 × 10⁻¹⁰; s = 1.8 × 10⁻⁹ mol/L. The common Cl⁻ shifts AgCl ⇌ Ag⁺ + Cl⁻ left (Le Chatelier), dramatically reducing solubility vs ~1.34 × 10⁻⁵ mol/L in pure water.

choices.text: Solubility in 0.10 M NaCl is the same as in pure water because K_sp is constant
choices.explanation: K_sp is constant, but solubility is not; the common ion (Cl⁻) from NaCl shifts the equilibrium and reduces AgCl solubility. Constant K_sp does not mean constant solubility.

choices.text: Solubility in 0.10 M NaCl is higher than in pure water because ionic strength increases solubility
choices.explanation: While ionic strength can slightly increase solubility (Debye-Hückel effects), the dominant and tested effect is the common ion effect (Cl⁻ from NaCl reduces AgCl solubility dramatically).

choices.text: AgCl cannot dissolve in NaCl solution because Cl⁻ reacts with Ag⁺ to form a complex
choices.explanation: While Ag⁺ can form complexes with excess Cl⁻ (AgCl₂⁻), the primary AP Chemistry answer for the common ion effect is that the equilibrium shifts left, reducing solubility.

questions.subject: ap-chemistry

questions.question: Which of the following correctly explains why the equilibrium constant $K$ has no units?

choices.text: K has no units because concentrations always cancel in the numerator and denominator
choices.explanation: Concentrations in K do not always cancel (e.g., for N₂ + 3H₂ ⇌ 2NH₃, K_c = [NH₃]²/([N₂][H₂]³) has net units of M⁻²). K is dimensionless by convention because each concentration is divided by a standard reference concentration.

choices.text: K is dimensionless because each concentration term is expressed as a ratio relative to a standard reference concentration (1 M for solutions, 1 atm for gases), making K a ratio of dimensionless quantities
choices.explanation: Thermodynamically, equilibrium constants use activities (dimensionless ratios). For dilute solutions, activity ≈ [A]/c° where c° = 1 M, making each term dimensionless. Similarly, for gases, activity ≈ P/P° where P° = 1 bar.

choices.text: K has no units because equilibrium applies only to gases, and gas concentrations are always dimensionless
choices.explanation: Equilibrium applies to solutions as well as gases; gas concentrations have units of mol/L or atm. The dimensionless nature of K comes from using relative activities.

choices.text: K has no units because it is calculated by taking the natural logarithm of concentrations
choices.explanation: K is not calculated by taking logarithms; it is a ratio of products to reactants raised to their stoichiometric powers. The dimensionlessness comes from the activity concept.

questions.subject: ap-chemistry

questions.question: Based on the data in the table, which of the following conclusions is best supported?

choices.text: All three silver halides have the same solubility because they have the same 1:1 formula
choices.explanation: The table clearly shows different solubilities; for 1:1 salts s = √K_sp, so larger K_sp gives larger molar solubility. AgCl (K_sp 10⁻¹⁰) is far more soluble than AgI (K_sp 10⁻¹⁷).

choices.text: As K_sp decreases, molar solubility decreases, because lower K_sp means less dissociation at equilibrium
choices.explanation: For 1:1 salts, s = √K_sp; smaller K_sp gives smaller s. The data confirms: K_sp = 1.8×10⁻¹⁰ (AgCl, s = 1.3×10⁻⁵) > K_sp = 1.1×10⁻¹² (AgBr, s = 1.0×10⁻⁶) > K_sp = 8.5×10⁻¹⁷ (AgI, s = 9.2×10⁻⁹).

choices.text: AgI is more soluble than AgCl because iodide is larger and creates weaker lattice energy
choices.explanation: The data show AgI is less soluble (smaller K_sp); while AgI does have lower lattice energy, the hydration energy also decreases, and the net result is AgI being less soluble.

choices.text: Molar solubility equals K_sp for all 1:1 salts
choices.explanation: For 1:1 salts MX: K_sp = [M⁺][X⁻] = s², so s = √K_sp, not s = K_sp. The table confirms this: s ≠ K_sp for any of the three salts.

questions.subject: ap-chemistry

questions.question: For the reaction $\text{A}(g) \rightleftharpoons \text{B}(g) + \text{C}(g)$, $K_c = 0.040$ at 500 K. Initially, only A is present at 1.0 M. Which of the following correctly applies an ICE table to find the equilibrium concentrations?

choices.text: ICE gives $x = 0.17$ M; $[\text{A}]_{eq} = 0.83$ M, $[\text{B}]_{eq} = [\text{C}]_{eq} = 0.17$ M
choices.explanation: ICE: [A] = 1.0 − x; [B] = [C] = x. K_c = x²/(1.0 − x) = 0.040. Assume 1.0 − x ≈ 1.0 (small x approximation): x² ≈ 0.040; x ≈ 0.20. Check: 0.20/1.0 = 20% (not small), so solve exactly: x² + 0.040x − 0.040 = 0. Using quadratic: x = [−0.040 + √(0.0016 + 0.16)]/2 ≈ 0.185/2... approximate gives x ≈ 0.17.

choices.text: ICE gives $x = 0.20$ M; $[\text{A}]_{eq} = 0.80$ M, $[\text{B}]_{eq} = [\text{C}]_{eq} = 0.20$ M
choices.explanation: x = 0.20 assumes the 5% approximation holds: K_c ≈ x²/1.0; x = √0.040 = 0.20. Check: 0.20²/(1.0 − 0.20) = 0.04/0.80 = 0.050 ≠ 0.040. The approximation fails; x ≈ 0.17 is more accurate from the quadratic.

choices.text: ICE gives $[\text{A}]_{eq} = 1.0$ M; equilibrium is reached immediately with no conversion
choices.explanation: K_c = 0.040 ≠ 0; some reaction occurs to establish equilibrium. [A] = 1.0 M is the initial concentration, not the equilibrium concentration.

choices.text: ICE gives $x = 1.0$ M; all A converts to B and C because K_c > 0
choices.explanation: K_c = 0.040 indicates an equilibrium that slightly favors reactants; it is not large enough to drive the reaction to completion. ICE gives a partial conversion, not complete conversion.

questions.subject: ap-chemistry

questions.question: Which of the following best explains why adding more solid to an equilibrium system involving a slightly soluble salt does not change the equilibrium concentrations of the dissolved ions?

choices.text: Solid compounds are not included in K_sp because their activity is defined as 1 (pure solid), so adding more solid does not change the equilibrium expression
choices.explanation: The activity of a pure solid is defined as 1 in thermodynamics; adding more solid does not change its activity. K_sp only involves aqueous ion concentrations, so additional solid does not shift the equilibrium.

choices.text: The solid dissolves immediately upon addition, canceling any equilibrium shift
choices.explanation: If the solution is already saturated at equilibrium, adding more solid does not cause it to dissolve further; the system is already at K_sp.

choices.text: Adding solid increases the pressure, which shifts the equilibrium back to cancel the effect
choices.explanation: Pressure effects apply to gas-phase equilibria. Dissolving solids in aqueous solution is essentially pressure-independent under normal conditions.

choices.text: Solid salts are always 100% insoluble, so they never affect ion concentrations
choices.explanation: The concept of K_sp applies to slightly soluble (not fully insoluble) salts; they do partially dissolve to give some ion concentrations. The point is that excess solid does not increase ion concentrations beyond what K_sp allows.

questions.subject: ap-chemistry

stimulus.content: Consider the reaction: $\text{A}(g) + \text{B}(g) \rightleftharpoons 2\text{C}(g)$, $\Delta H = -80$ kJ. At equilibrium, the system is subjected to the following changes:

questions.question: Which of the following changes will shift the equilibrium toward the products (right)?

choices.text: Increasing temperature
choices.explanation: The reaction is exothermic; heat is a product. Increasing temperature shifts equilibrium left (toward reactants) to absorb the added heat.

choices.text: Removing product C
choices.explanation: Removing a product decreases the concentration of C, causing Q < K; the system responds by shifting right to produce more C until equilibrium is re-established.

choices.text: Increasing the volume of the container
choices.explanation: The reaction has equal moles of gas on both sides (2 mol A + B on left, 2 mol C on right); a change in volume/pressure has no net effect on the equilibrium position for this reaction.

choices.text: Adding a catalyst
choices.explanation: A catalyst increases the rate of both forward and reverse reactions equally; it does not shift the equilibrium position or change the value of K.

questions.subject: ap-chemistry

questions.question: For the equilibrium $2\text{NO}(g) + \text{O}_2(g) \rightleftharpoons 2\text{NO}_2(g)$, which of the following expressions correctly represents $K_p$?

choices.text: $K_p = \frac{P_{\text{NO}_2}^2}{P_{\text{NO}}^2 \cdot P_{\text{O}_2}}$
choices.explanation: K_p uses partial pressures raised to stoichiometric coefficients: products in numerator, reactants in denominator. For $2\text{NO} + \text{O}_2 \rightleftharpoons 2\text{NO}_2$: $K_p = P_{\text{NO}_2}^2 / (P_{\text{NO}}^2 \cdot P_{\text{O}_2})$.

choices.text: $K_p = \frac{P_{\text{NO}}^2 \cdot P_{\text{O}_2}}{P_{\text{NO}_2}^2}$
choices.explanation: This is the K_p expression for the reverse reaction; the forward reaction (products in numerator) gives K_p = P(NO₂)²/[P(NO)²·P(O₂)].

choices.text: $K_p = \frac{2P_{\text{NO}_2}}{2P_{\text{NO}} + P_{\text{O}_2}}$
choices.explanation: Stoichiometric coefficients are exponents, not multipliers, in equilibrium expressions; the correct form uses pressures raised to their coefficients.

choices.text: $K_p = K_c \cdot RT$
choices.explanation: The relationship between K_p and K_c is $K_p = K_c(RT)^{\Delta n}$ where Δn = change in moles of gas; for this reaction Δn = 2 − (2+1) = −1, so $K_p = K_c(RT)^{-1}$.

questions.subject: ap-chemistry

questions.question: A student mixes 50.0 mL of $2.0 \times 10^{-5}$ M AgNO₃ with 50.0 mL of $2.0 \times 10^{-5}$ M NaCl. Will a precipitate of AgCl form? ($K_{sp}$(AgCl) = $1.8 \times 10^{-10}$)

choices.text: No precipitate forms because both concentrations are very low
choices.explanation: Whether a precipitate forms depends on comparing Q_sp to K_sp; low concentrations do not automatically prevent precipitation if Q > K_sp.

choices.text: No precipitate forms; Q < K_sp
choices.explanation: After mixing, each concentration is halved: [Ag⁺] = [Cl⁻] = 1.0 × 10⁻⁵ M. Q_sp = (1.0 × 10⁻⁵)(1.0 × 10⁻⁵) = 1.0 × 10⁻¹⁰ < K_sp (1.8 × 10⁻¹⁰). Since Q < K_sp, the solution is unsaturated and no precipitate forms.

choices.text: A precipitate forms; Q > K_sp
choices.explanation: Q_sp = (1.0×10⁻⁵)(1.0×10⁻⁵) = 1.0×10⁻¹⁰; K_sp = 1.8×10⁻¹⁰. Since Q < K_sp, the solution is below saturation and no precipitate forms.

choices.text: A precipitate forms only if the temperature is raised above 25°C
choices.explanation: Temperature affects K_sp (it increases for most salts with increasing temperature), but the Q vs K_sp comparison at 25°C shows Q < K_sp; the relevant comparison is Q vs K_sp at the given temperature.

questions.subject: ap-chemistry

questions.question: Which of the following correctly relates $K_p$ and $K_c$ for the reaction $\text{N}_2(g) + 3\text{H}_2(g) \rightleftharpoons 2\text{NH}_3(g)$?

choices.explanation: Δn = moles gas products − moles gas reactants = 2 − (1+3) = −2; the relationship is $K_p = K_c(RT)^{\Delta n} = K_c(RT)^{-2}$, not $(RT)^2$.
$\Delta n_{gas} = 2 - (1+3) = -2$. Therefore $K_p = K_c(RT)^{-2} = K_c/(RT)^2$. Since Δn < 0, K_p < K_c for this reaction at most temperatures.

choices.text: $K_p = K_c$, because all species are gases
choices.explanation: K_p = K_c only when Δn_gas = 0; here Δn_gas = −2, so K_p ≠ K_c.
Δn = −2, not +4; even summing |reactant moles| + |product moles| = 4 is not the correct calculation. Δn = products − reactants = 2 − 4 = −2.

questions.subject: ap-chemistry

stimulus.content: A student observes that when HCl gas is added to a saturated solution of NaCl, a white precipitate of NaCl appears. She knows K_sp of NaCl is quite large (NaCl is very soluble), yet precipitation still occurs.

questions.question: Which of the following best explains this observation using equilibrium principles?

choices.text: The common ion effect: adding Cl⁻ from HCl increases the ion product $Q_{sp} = [\text{Na}^+][\text{Cl}^-]$ above K_sp, causing precipitation
choices.explanation: Even though K_sp(NaCl) is large, the saturated solution already has [Na⁺][Cl⁻] = K_sp. Adding Cl⁻ from HCl increases [Cl⁻], making Q_sp > K_sp; to restore equilibrium, NaCl precipitates until Q_sp returns to K_sp.

choices.text: HCl reacts with NaCl to form NaHCl₂, a less soluble compound
choices.explanation: NaHCl₂ does not exist as a stable compound; the precipitation is of NaCl itself due to the common ion effect from added Cl⁻.

choices.text: HCl lowers the pH, which decreases the solubility of all ionic compounds
choices.explanation: pH (H⁺ concentration) does not directly affect the solubility of NaCl; solubility of basic anions (like S²⁻, CO₃²⁻) is pH-dependent, but Cl⁻ is not.

choices.text: Temperature decreases when HCl is added, lowering K_sp and causing precipitation
choices.explanation: Adding HCl gas does not significantly lower the temperature; the precipitation is driven by the common ion effect (Q > K_sp), not temperature change.

questions.subject: ap-chemistry

questions.question: For a reversible reaction at equilibrium, which of the following best describes what happens when an inert gas is added at constant volume?

choices.text: The total pressure increases, shifting the equilibrium toward the side with fewer moles of gas
choices.explanation: Adding an inert gas at constant volume does not change the partial pressures (or concentrations) of the reacting gases; only total pressure increases. Since K depends on partial pressures, not total pressure, the equilibrium is unaffected.

choices.text: The partial pressures and mole fractions of all reacting gases decrease, shifting the equilibrium toward the side with more moles of gas
choices.explanation: At constant volume, adding an inert gas does not change the partial pressures of the reacting gases (PV = nRT; partial pressure depends only on moles of that specific gas and volume). Equilibrium is unaffected.

choices.text: The equilibrium is unaffected because the partial pressures (and concentrations) of the reacting gases remain unchanged
choices.explanation: At constant volume, the partial pressure of each reacting gas depends on its own moles and the volume; adding an inert gas does not change either. K_c and K_p involve only reacting species, so the equilibrium position does not shift.

choices.text: Adding an inert gas always favors the reverse reaction because the increased pressure favors fewer particles
choices.explanation: This would apply if the volume changed (e.g., compression), not if an inert gas is added at constant volume. At constant volume, inert gas addition does not shift equilibrium.

questions.subject: ap-chemistry

questions.question: Which of the following correctly explains the difference between Q and K in a chemical equilibrium?

choices.text: Q is the equilibrium constant at high temperature; K is the equilibrium constant at low temperature
choices.explanation: Q is the reaction quotient calculated using actual (non-equilibrium) concentrations at any point in the reaction; K is the equilibrium constant using equilibrium concentrations. Both Q and K can be calculated at any temperature.

choices.text: Q is the same expression as K but calculated using the actual concentrations at any given moment; comparing Q to K predicts the direction of reaction
choices.explanation: Q uses the same ratio as K (products/reactants) but is calculated at the current concentrations, which may or may not be at equilibrium. If Q < K, reaction proceeds forward; Q > K, reaction goes reverse; Q = K, system is at equilibrium.

choices.text: K is always larger than Q because K represents the maximum possible product concentration
choices.explanation: K is not always larger than Q; Q can be greater than K (when too much product is present). The relative values of Q and K determine reaction direction.

choices.text: Q and K are the same quantity but expressed in different units
choices.explanation: Q and K use the same expression and the same units (both dimensionless); they differ in that Q uses actual concentrations and K uses equilibrium concentrations.

questions.subject: ap-chemistry

stimulus.content: A student is given: $K_{sp}(\text{BaSO}_4) = 1.1 \times 10^{-10}$. She measures $[\text{Ba}^{2+}] = 5 \times 10^{-5}$ M and $[\text{SO}_4^{2-}] = 5 \times 10^{-5}$ M in a solution.

questions.question: Which of the following best predicts whether BaSO₄ will precipitate?

choices.text: $Q_{sp} = 2.5 \times 10^{-9} > K_{sp}$; BaSO₄ will precipitate
choices.explanation: $Q_{sp} = [\text{Ba}^{2+}][\text{SO}_4^{2-}] = (5 \times 10^{-5})(5 \times 10^{-5}) = 2.5 \times 10^{-9}$. Since $Q_{sp} = 2.5 \times 10^{-9} > K_{sp} = 1.1 \times 10^{-10}$, the solution is supersaturated and BaSO₄ will precipitate.

choices.text: $Q_{sp} = 2.5 \times 10^{-9} < K_{sp}$; no precipitate forms
choices.explanation: K_sp = 1.1 × 10⁻¹⁰; Q_sp = 2.5 × 10⁻⁹. Since 2.5 × 10⁻⁹ > 1.1 × 10⁻¹⁰, Q > K_sp and BaSO₄ precipitates.

choices.text: $Q_{sp} = 1.0 \times 10^{-4} > K_{sp}$; BaSO₄ will precipitate
choices.explanation: Q_sp = [Ba²⁺][SO₄²⁻] = (5×10⁻⁵)(5×10⁻⁵) = 25×10⁻¹⁰ = 2.5×10⁻⁹, not 10⁻⁴; the direction of the prediction (precipitation) is correct, but the Q value is wrong.

choices.text: No prediction can be made without knowing the temperature
choices.explanation: K_sp is given at 25°C; if the measurement is also at 25°C, the comparison is valid. The relative magnitudes of Q_sp and K_sp clearly predict precipitation.

questions.subject: ap-chemistry

questions.question: For the exothermic reaction $\text{A} \rightleftharpoons \text{B}$, which of the following correctly describes how the equilibrium constant K changes with temperature?

choices.text: K increases as temperature increases because more collisions occur, producing more B
choices.explanation: More collisions affect reaction rate, not the equilibrium constant. For an exothermic reaction, K decreases as temperature increases (product formation is disfavored at high T).

choices.text: K decreases as temperature increases because the exothermic forward reaction is disfavored at higher temperatures, shifting equilibrium toward reactants
choices.explanation: For exothermic reactions, heat is a product; increasing temperature is like adding a product (Le Chatelier), shifting equilibrium left. This reduces [B]/[A] at equilibrium, decreasing K. Thermodynamically, ln K = −ΔH°/RT + ΔS°/R; for ΔH° < 0 (exothermic), increasing T decreases ln K (decreases K).

choices.text: K is independent of temperature; only concentrations and pressure change K
choices.explanation: Temperature is the only variable that changes K; concentration and pressure changes shift equilibrium but do not change K. This is a fundamental principle of equilibrium.

choices.text: K first decreases and then increases as temperature rises, following a non-linear pattern
choices.explanation: For a reaction with constant ΔH°, K varies monotonically with temperature; for exothermic reactions, K decreases continuously as T increases.

questions.subject: ap-chemistry

stimulus.content: A student measures the equilibrium concentrations for $\text{H}_2(g) + \text{Br}_2(g) \rightleftharpoons 2\text{HBr}(g)$: $[\text{H}_2] = 0.20$ M, $[\text{Br}_2] = 0.30$ M, $[\text{HBr}] = 0.60$ M.

questions.question: Which of the following correctly calculates $K_c$ and interprets the value?

choices.text: $K_c = 6.0$; products and reactants are roughly equal at equilibrium
choices.explanation: $K_c = [\text{HBr}]^2 / ([\text{H}_2][\text{Br}_2]) = (0.60)^2 / (0.20 \times 0.30) = 0.36 / 0.060 = 6.0$. K = 6.0 is modest; neither side strongly dominates, though products are slightly favored.

choices.text: $K_c = 0.167$; reactants are strongly favored
choices.explanation: 0.167 = 1/6 which is the reciprocal of K (i.e., K for the reverse reaction); the forward K_c = 6.0, not 0.167.

choices.text: $K_c = 36$; products are strongly favored
choices.explanation: 36 = (0.60)² / (0.20 × 0.30) / 0.06... let me check: 0.36/0.060 = 6.0. K_c = 6.0, not 36; 36 would result from squaring (0.60)² = 0.36 and dividing by 0.010 (not 0.060).

choices.text: $K_c = 60$; the forward reaction is essentially complete
choices.explanation: 60 would result from a calculation error; K_c = (0.60)²/(0.20 × 0.30) = 0.36/0.060 = 6.0. The reaction is not essentially complete.

questions.subject: ap-chemistry

questions.question: Which of the following correctly explains the concept of dynamic equilibrium?

choices.text: At dynamic equilibrium, the forward reaction has stopped and only the reverse reaction continues
choices.explanation: At dynamic equilibrium, both forward and reverse reactions continue to occur; the key feature is that the rates are equal, not that one has stopped.

choices.text: At dynamic equilibrium, both forward and reverse reactions occur at the same rate, resulting in constant macroscopic concentrations even though reactions continue at the molecular level
choices.explanation: Dynamic equilibrium means the system appears static (constant concentrations) at the macroscopic level, but at the molecular level, reactants are continuously converting to products and products are converting back at equal rates.

choices.text: Dynamic equilibrium means the concentrations of all species are equal at equilibrium
choices.explanation: Equal concentrations would require K = 1 for a 1:1 reaction; K can have any value. Dynamic equilibrium means constant concentrations (not necessarily equal) due to equal forward and reverse rates.

choices.text: Dynamic equilibrium only applies to homogeneous reactions; heterogeneous reactions reach static (true) equilibrium
choices.explanation: Dynamic equilibrium applies to all reversible reactions, including heterogeneous ones (e.g., CaCO₃(s) ⇌ CaO(s) + CO₂(g)). The dynamic nature of equilibrium is universal.

questions.subject: ap-chemistry

stimulus.content: The industrial synthesis of ammonia uses: $\text{N}_2(g) + 3\text{H}_2(g) \rightleftharpoons 2\text{NH}_3(g)$, $\Delta H = -92$ kJ/mol. The process is run at 400-500°C and 150-300 atm with an iron catalyst.

questions.question: Which of the following best explains why the industrial Haber process uses high pressure despite the associated engineering costs?

choices.text: High pressure increases the temperature, which increases the rate of the forward reaction
choices.explanation: Pressure and temperature are independent variables; increasing pressure does not directly increase temperature.

choices.text: High pressure shifts the equilibrium toward NH₃ because the forward reaction produces fewer moles of gas (2 mol) from more moles of gas (4 mol)
choices.explanation: The forward reaction reduces 4 mol of gas to 2 mol; Le Chatelier's principle predicts that increased pressure favors the side with fewer moles of gas (products). High pressure increases the equilibrium yield of NH₃ significantly.

choices.text: High pressure lowers the activation energy for the forward reaction, increasing the rate
choices.explanation: Activation energy is lowered by catalysts (iron), not by pressure; pressure affects equilibrium position, not activation energy.

choices.text: High pressure prevents the catalyst from deactivating at high temperatures
choices.explanation: Catalyst stability is addressed by the choice of catalyst material and promoters, not by the operating pressure.

questions.subject: ap-chemistry

questions.question: For the equilibrium $\text{FeO}(s) + \text{CO}(g) \rightleftharpoons \text{Fe}(s) + \text{CO}_2(g)$, which of the following correctly writes $K_c$?

choices.text: $K_c = \frac{[\text{Fe}][\text{CO}_2]}{[\text{FeO}][\text{CO}]}$
choices.explanation: Pure solids (Fe and FeO) are not included in the equilibrium expression because their activities are defined as 1.

choices.text: $K_c = \frac{[\text{CO}_2]}{[\text{CO}]}$
choices.explanation: For heterogeneous equilibria, pure solids (FeO and Fe) are omitted from the K expression (their activities = 1). Only the gaseous species appear: $K_c = [\text{CO}_2]/[\text{CO}]$.

choices.text: $K_c = [\text{CO}_2][\text{CO}]$
choices.explanation: K_c has products in the numerator and reactants in the denominator; CO is a reactant, not a product, and should be in the denominator.

choices.text: $K_c = [\text{CO}]/[\text{CO}_2]$
choices.explanation: This is the K_c for the reverse reaction; for the forward reaction (products / reactants), CO₂ (product) is in the numerator and CO (reactant) is in the denominator.

questions.subject: ap-chemistry
